topic: The Skin as an Organ, Primary & Secondary Lesions

cards.q: What is the integumentary system?
cards.a: The skin and its accessory organs — hair, nails and glands.

cards.q: What does the word 'integument' mean?
cards.a: Covering. The skin is the outer covering of the body.

cards.q: Name the accessory organs of the skin.
cards.a: Hair, nails and glands.

cards.q: Your lecturer says the skin is 'more than a simple body covering'. What three specialized components does she list?
cards.a: Glands that secrete several types of fluids; nerves that carry impulses; and blood vessels that aid in the regulation of body temperature.

cards.q: Why is the skin often referred to as the largest body organ?
cards.a: Because it covers the entire body surface and serves as the main protective barrier against damage to internal tissues.

cards.q: Against what five things does the skin protect internal tissues?
cards.a: Trauma, ultraviolet light, temperature, toxins and bacteria.

cards.q: List the four functions of the skin.
cards.a: Protection, secretion, sensation and thermoregulation.

cards.q: As a protective membrane, what does the skin guard the deeper tissues against?
cards.a: Excessive loss of water, salts and heat; and invasion of pathogens and their toxins.

cards.q: Name the two types of glands in the skin and their secretions.
cards.a: Sebaceous glands produce an oily secretion called sebum; sweat glands produce a watery secretion called sweat.

cards.q: What is the function of sebum?
cards.a: It helps to lubricate the surface of the skin.

cards.q: How does sweat help the body?
cards.a: It helps to cool the body as it evaporates from the skin surface.

cards.q: What sensations do the nerve fibres under the skin act as receptors for?
cards.a: Pain, temperature, pressure and touch.

cards.q: What is thermoregulation in the context of the skin?
cards.a: Several different tissues in the skin aid in maintaining the body temperature.

cards.q: Name the three layers of the skin from the outer surface inwards.
cards.a: 1. Epidermis; 2. Corium or dermis; 3. Subcutaneous tissue or hypodermis.

cards.q: How does your lecturer describe the epidermis?
cards.a: A thin cellular membrane layer.

cards.q: How does your lecturer describe the dermis?
cards.a: A dense, fibrous connective tissue layer.

cards.q: How does your lecturer describe the subcutaneous tissue?
cards.a: A thick, fat-containing tissue.

cards.q: What is another name for the dermis?
cards.a: The corium.

cards.q: What are the other names for the hypodermis?
cards.a: Subcutaneous tissue or subcutis.

cards.q: In which direction do cells move within the epidermis, and what happens to them?
cards.a: Cells move from the base of the epidermis up to the surface, changing shape and structure as they go.

cards.q: Name the five layers of the epidermis from upper to lower.
cards.a: Stratum corneum; granular layer; spinous layer; basal layer; basement membrane.

cards.q: What is the stratum corneum made of, and what is its role?
cards.a: Stratified squamous epithelium or hardened cells, which play a role in the skin's protective function.

cards.q: Describe the granular layer.
cards.a: It is the transitional layer where keratinocyte skin cells develop into their final form and die. It contains keratohyalin and lamellar granules.

cards.q: Which two granules are found in the granular layer?
cards.a: Keratohyalin and lamellar granules.

cards.q: Describe the spinous layer.
cards.a: Keratin-producing epidermal cells that provide a continuous net-like layer of protection for underlying tissue.

cards.q: What special cells are found in the basal layer?
cards.a: Melanocytes.

cards.q: What do melanocytes form and contain?
cards.a: A black pigment called melanin.

cards.q: Is the number of melanocytes different among the races?
cards.a: No — the number of melanocytes in all races is the same.

cards.q: What accounts for the colour differences among the races?
cards.a: The amount of melanin, not the number of melanocytes.

cards.q: What is the function of the basement membrane?
cards.a: It connects and functionally separates the epidermis and the dermis.

cards.q: What three things does the epidermis NOT contain?
cards.a: Blood vessels, lymphatic vessels and connective tissue (elastic fibres, cartilage, fat).

cards.q: How is the epidermis nourished?
cards.a: It is dependent on the deeper dermis layer and its rich network of capillaries for nourishment.

cards.q: What is the dermis composed of?
cards.a: Blood and lymph vessels and nerve fibres, as well as the accessory organs of the skin.

cards.q: Which accessory organs of the skin are located in the dermis?
cards.a: The hair follicles, sweat glands and sebaceous glands.

cards.q: What is the main function of the dermis?
cards.a: To provide physical support and nutrients to the epidermis.

cards.q: Name the two layers of the dermis.
cards.a: The papillary layer and the reticular layer.

cards.q: What does the papillary dermis contain?
cards.a: Smaller blood vessels which supply oxygen, elastic fibres and nutrients to the lower epidermis.

cards.q: What does the reticular dermis contain?
cards.a: Dense connective tissue, larger blood vessels, elastic fibres, and bundles of collagen arranged in layers.

cards.q: Which is the thicker layer of the dermis?
cards.a: The reticular dermis.

cards.q: Name the five key cell types or structures within the reticular layer.
cards.a: Fibroblasts; mast cells; lymphatic vessels; epidermal appendages or rete pegs; and ground substance.

cards.q: What is the role of fibroblasts?
cards.a: A key cell involved in repairing tissue damage.

cards.q: What is the role of mast cells in the dermis?
cards.a: They are involved in fighting infection.

cards.q: What is the role of lymphatic vessels in the dermis?
cards.a: The lymphatic system is a key part of the body's defence against infection.

cards.q: What are rete pegs and what do they do?
cards.a: Epidermal appendages that link the epidermis and dermis together to prevent skin damage.

cards.q: What is ground substance?
cards.a: A gel-like substance that helps to support the cells within the dermis and provides structure to the area.

cards.q: What is the subcutaneous layer composed of?
cards.a: Adipose tissue or fat tissue.

cards.q: State the two functions of the hypodermis.
cards.a: Protection of the deeper tissues of the body, and acting as a heat insulator.

cards.q: Define a skin lesion.
cards.a: Any skin area that has different characteristics from the surrounding skin, including colour, shape, size and texture.

cards.q: Give two examples of localized damage that produce skin lesions.
cards.a: Sunburns and contact dermatitis.

cards.q: Skin lesions can be manifestations of which underlying disorders?
cards.a: Underlying disorders such as diabetes and infections.

cards.q: How are skin lesions classified?
cards.a: Into primary lesions and secondary lesions.

cards.q: Define a primary skin lesion.
cards.a: One that originates on previously healthy skin and is directly associated with a specific cause.

cards.q: List the examples of primary skin lesions your lecturer gives.
cards.a: Bullae, burrow, macule, nodule, papule, plaque, pustule, tumour, vesicle and wheal.

cards.q: Define a secondary skin lesion.
cards.a: One that develops from the evolution of a primary skin lesion, either due to traumatic manipulation such as scratching or rubbing, or due to its treatment or progression.

cards.q: Name the three ways a primary lesion becomes a secondary lesion.
cards.a: Traumatic manipulation (scratching, rubbing); treatment; and progression.

cards.q: List the examples of secondary skin lesions your lecturer gives.
cards.a: Crusts, sores, ulcers and scars.

cards.q: Define a vesicle.
cards.a: A raised lesion filled with clear fluid that is <1 cm in diameter.

cards.q: Define a bulla.
cards.a: A raised lesion filled with clear fluid >1 cm in diameter.

cards.q: What is the only difference between a vesicle and a bulla?
cards.a: Size — vesicle is <1 cm, bulla is >1 cm. Both are raised and filled with clear fluid.

cards.q: Define a papule.
cards.a: A circumscribed palpable elevation with distinct borders <0.5 cm in diameter.

cards.q: Define a plaque.
cards.a: An elevated area of skin, with distinct borders and an epidermal change, >1 cm in diameter.

cards.q: Which primary lesion uses a 0.5 cm cut-off rather than 1 cm?
cards.a: The papule.

cards.q: Define a macule.
cards.a: A circumscribed alteration in the colour of the skin that is flat to the surface of the skin and not palpable, <1 cm in size.

cards.q: Define a patch.
cards.a: A large macule, >1 cm in size.

cards.q: Which two words in the definition of a macule tell you it cannot be felt?
cards.a: 'Flat to the surface of the skin' and 'not palpable'.

cards.q: Define a nodule.
cards.a: A circumscribed raised solid lesion ≤1 cm.

cards.q: Define a wheal.
cards.a: A transient area of dermal or dermal and hypodermal oedema, compressible and usually evanescent.

cards.q: Which primary lesion is defined partly by TIME?
cards.a: The wheal — it is transient and usually evanescent.

cards.q: What does 'evanescent' mean?
cards.a: That the lesion vanishes — it disappears, usually without leaving a mark.

cards.q: Why is a wheal compressible?
cards.a: Because it is made of oedema fluid in the dermis, which can be pushed aside.

cards.q: Define a burrow.
cards.a: A small linear tunnel in the skin that houses a parasite such as scabies.

cards.q: Which primary lesion's definition names its cause?
cards.a: The burrow — its definition states it houses a parasite.

cards.q: Define a pustule.
cards.a: A circumscribed elevation of pus-filled skin lesion <1 cm.

cards.q: What separates a vesicle from a pustule?
cards.a: The contents — a vesicle contains clear fluid, a pustule contains pus.

cards.q: Define a crust.
cards.a: A hard, friable irregular layer of dried blood, serum, pus, tissue debris or any combination of these, adherent to the surface of injured or inflamed skin.

cards.q: Define an ulcer.
cards.a: An open sore or erosion of the skin or mucous membrane.

cards.q: Define scaling.
cards.a: Whitish plates or flakes of stratum corneum present on the skin surface.

cards.q: Define a scar.
cards.a: Fibrous tissue replacing normal tissue destroyed by injury or disease.

cards.q: Define erosion and oozing.
cards.a: Moist, circumscribed, slightly depressed areas representing a blister base with the roof of the blister removed.

cards.q: Define desquamation, and give the examples your lecturer gives.
cards.a: The peeling of sheets of scale after an acute injury to skin — burns, toxic drug reaction.

cards.q: Define excoriation, and give the example your lecturer gives.
cards.a: Oval to linear depressions in the skin with a complete removal of the epidermis, exposing a broad section of the red dermis — atopic dermatitis.

cards.q: Define a fissure, and give the example your lecturer gives.
cards.a: Linear, wedge-shaped cracks in the epidermis extending down to the dermis and narrowing at the base — warts.

cards.q: Define lichenification.
cards.a: A thickening of the epidermis (and to some extent also of the dermis) in response to prolonged rubbing.

cards.q: Define gangrene.
cards.a: A death of tissue, usually due to loss of blood supply.

cards.q: Which two secondary lesions are described as linear?
cards.a: Excoriation (oval to linear depressions) and fissure (linear, wedge-shaped cracks).

cards.q: What is the difference between scaling and desquamation?
cards.a: Scaling is whitish plates or flakes of stratum corneum on the surface; desquamation is the peeling of sheets of scale after acute injury to the skin.

cards.q: What is the difference between an erosion and an excoriation?
cards.a: An erosion is a blister base with the roof removed; an excoriation is complete removal of the epidermis by scratching, exposing red dermis.

cards.q: Which secondary lesion tells you the lesion itches?
cards.a: Excoriation — it is produced by scratching.

cards.q: Which secondary lesion tells you the process is chronic?
cards.a: Lichenification — it results from prolonged rubbing.

cards.q: Which layer of the skin is important as a heat insulator?
cards.a: The subcutaneous layer / hypodermis, being composed of adipose tissue.

cards.q: Which layer contains no blood vessels?
cards.a: The epidermis.

cards.q: Which epidermal layer is the only one that contains melanocytes?
cards.a: The basal layer.

cards.q: Which epidermal layer contains dead, hardened cells?
cards.a: The stratum corneum.

cards.q: Which epidermal layer is described as the transitional layer where cells die?
cards.a: The granular layer.

cards.q: Which layer of the dermis supplies the lower epidermis?
cards.a: The papillary dermis, through its smaller blood vessels.

cards.q: OSPE — What is the golden rule when shown a photograph of a skin lesion?
cards.a: Describe before you diagnose. Marks are awarded for accurate descriptive terminology even if the final diagnosis is wrong.

cards.q: OSPE — What is the first question to ask of any skin lesion?
cards.a: Is it flat or raised? If raised, what is inside it — clear fluid, pus, solid tissue, or oedema?

cards.q: OSPE — Give the ten-step sequence for describing a skin lesion.
cards.a: Type of lesion; flat or raised and contents; size; colour; shape and border; surface change; arrangement; distribution; secondary changes; then diagnosis.

cards.q: OSPE — Which size cut-offs must you quote when describing lesions?
cards.a: 0.5 cm for the papule; 1 cm for vesicle/bulla, macule/patch, nodule and pustule.

cards.q: OSPE — What does a LINEAR arrangement of lesions suggest?
cards.a: Something external travelled or was applied along that line — a burrow (scabies), dermographism, or a contact reaction.

cards.q: OSPE — You see a raised lesion but cannot palpate the photograph. How do you tell it is not a macule?
cards.a: Raised lesions cast a shadow and distort the surface light; a macule is a pure colour change with no shadow and no texture alteration.

cards.q: OSPE — A lesion is raised, well-demarcated, >1 cm, with silvery scale on the extensor surfaces. Name the lesion and likely diagnosis.
cards.a: The lesion is a plaque (elevated, distinct borders, epidermal change, >1 cm). The likely diagnosis is psoriasis.

cards.q: OSPE — You are shown a rash with macules, vesicles and crusts present simultaneously on the trunk and face. What is the diagnosis and what is the clinching feature?
cards.a: Chickenpox (varicella). The clinching feature is lesions at different stages of evolution present at the same time, appearing in crops, with a centripetal distribution.

cards.q: OSPE — How do you distinguish chickenpox from smallpox on lesion staging?
cards.a: In chickenpox lesions are at different stages; in smallpox all lesions were at the same stage. Chickenpox is centripetal (trunk and face), smallpox was centrifugal (face and limbs).

cards.q: OSPE — What is the single test that must never be omitted over a hypopigmented patch, and why?
cards.a: Test sensation with cotton wool, eyes closed, comparing with adjacent normal skin. An anaesthetic hypopigmented patch is tuberculoid leprosy.

cards.q: OSPE — Give four differentials for a hypopigmented macule or patch in a child.
cards.a: Pityriasis alba; post-inflammatory hypopigmentation; vitiligo; pityriasis versicolor; and tuberculoid leprosy.

cards.q: OSPE — How does vitiligo differ in appearance from pityriasis alba?
cards.a: Vitiligo is completely depigmented (chalk white) with a sharp border; pityriasis alba is only partially hypopigmented with an ill-defined border and fine scale.

cards.q: OSPE — You are shown raised, pink, linear coalescing lesions produced by stroking the skin minutes earlier. What is this?
cards.a: Wheals due to dermographism — a form of physical/inducible urticaria.

cards.q: OSPE — Describe the triple response of Lewis.
cards.a: Mechanical stimulation causes mast cell degranulation and histamine release, producing a red line (vasodilatation), a wheal (increased vascular permeability with dermal oedema), and a surrounding flare (axon reflex vasodilatation).

cards.q: OSPE — Which cell listed in the anatomy of the reticular dermis is responsible for producing a wheal?
cards.a: The mast cell — it degranulates and releases histamine.

cards.q: OSPE — A fine greyish wavy line 5 mm long with a dark speck at one end is seen in a finger web space. Identify it.
cards.a: A burrow — the dark speck is the female Sarcoptes scabiei mite at the advancing end of her tunnel. This is pathognomonic of scabies.

cards.q: OSPE — Where do you look for scabies burrows in an older child versus an infant?
cards.a: Older child: finger web spaces, flexor wrists, axillae, umbilicus, waistband and genitalia. In infants also the palms, soles, scalp and face.

cards.q: OSPE — Why is scabies itch classically worse at night?
cards.a: The skin warms under bedclothes, the mite becomes more active, and there are fewer distractions.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Outline the treatment of scabies in a child.
cards.a: Permethrin 5% cream to the whole body from the neck down (including head, face, palms and soles in infants), left 8–14 hours then washed off, repeated after 7 days; or benzyl benzoate if permethrin is unavailable. Add an oral antihistamine, treat secondary bacterial infection, treat all household contacts simultaneously, and hot-wash and sun-dry clothing and bedding.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why must scabies treatment be repeated after 7 days?
cards.a: The scabicide kills mites but eggs are more resistant. Eggs hatch in 3–4 days, so the second application kills the newly hatched generation before it can lay.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why must all household contacts be treated even if asymptomatic?
cards.a: An untreated contact is a reservoir; the child will be reinfested and the treatment will appear to have failed.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A parent returns saying the itch persists 2 weeks after permethrin. Has treatment failed?
cards.a: Not necessarily — post-scabetic itch is an allergic response to dead mite antigen and can persist for 2–4 weeks after successful treatment. Warn parents about this in advance.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why does secondary bacterial infection of scabies matter beyond the skin?
cards.a: Streptococcal skin infection is a recognised trigger for post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis, so treating superinfected scabies protects the kidneys.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A 6-year-old has an itchy flexural rash with papules, excoriations and lichenification. What is the diagnosis and what does each secondary lesion tell you?
cards.a: Atopic dermatitis. The excoriations tell you it itches; the lichenification tells you it has been present for weeks to months.

cards.q: CLINICAL — How does the distribution of atopic dermatitis change with age?
cards.a: In infants it affects the face and extensor surfaces; in older children it becomes flexural (antecubital and popliteal fossae).

cards.q: CLINICAL — Describe the itch–scratch cycle in atopic dermatitis.
cards.a: Itch leads to scratching, which produces excoriation and breaks the epidermal barrier, causing more irritation and more itch, and eventually lichenification from prolonged rubbing.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Using the anatomy of the skin, explain why emollients are first-line in atopic dermatitis.
cards.a: The defect is a failed stratum corneum barrier, which allows water loss outwards and irritants and allergens inwards. Emollients physically replace that barrier function.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A febrile child has red macules. What single bedside test must you do and why?
cards.a: Press the lesion or roll a glass over it. A non-blanching macule means blood outside the vessels — petechiae or purpura — and in a febrile child this is meningococcaemia until proven otherwise.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Distinguish blanching from non-blanching red macules.
cards.a: Blanching means blood is still inside dilated vessels (erythema); non-blanching means blood has leaked outside the vessels (petechiae/purpura).

cards.q: CLINICAL — Give four causes of desquamation in a sick child.
cards.a: Burns; toxic drug reaction; scarlet fever; Kawasaki disease; staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome; Stevens-Johnson syndrome / toxic epidermal necrolysis.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A child with sickle cell anaemia has a blackened, cold, dead toe. Name the lesion and explain the mechanism.
cards.a: Gangrene — death of tissue due to loss of blood supply. Sickled erythrocytes cause vaso-occlusion, leading to ischaemia, infarction and tissue death.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why does an ulcer scar but an erosion does not?
cards.a: Scarring requires dermal damage. An erosion involves the epidermis only, so it heals without a scar; an ulcer extends into the dermis, where fibroblasts lay down collagen to replace destroyed tissue.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Which dermal cell is responsible for scar formation, and where does it live?
cards.a: The fibroblast, which lives in the reticular layer of the dermis.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why is a preterm or malnourished child at high risk of hypothermia, in terms of skin anatomy?
cards.a: They have very little subcutaneous adipose tissue (the heat insulator) and a large surface area relative to body weight, so heat is lost rapidly.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Give three sterile causes of pustules.
cards.a: Acne; pustular psoriasis; and erythema toxicum neonatorum.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why should a well newborn with sterile pustules not automatically receive antibiotics?
cards.a: Erythema toxicum neonatorum produces sterile pustules in a well baby and resolves spontaneously — pus does not always mean infection.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Which primary lesion should prompt you to examine the lips, tongue, eyelids and airway?
cards.a: The wheal — because the same histamine-driven process in deeper or airway tissue is angioedema and anaphylaxis.

cards.q: CLINICAL — In which condition does the definition of the lesion itself give you the diagnosis?
cards.a: The burrow — its definition states that it houses a parasite such as scabies, making it pathognomonic.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why is erythema harder to appreciate in darkly pigmented skin, and what should you examine instead?
cards.a: Increased melanin masks the colour change. Examine the palms, soles, conjunctivae and mucous membranes instead.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A child has honey-coloured crusts on the face. What is the likely diagnosis and which lesion type is the crust?
cards.a: Impetigo. The crust is a secondary lesion — dried serum, pus and debris adherent to the surface of inflamed skin.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why can partial-thickness burns regenerate skin while full-thickness burns cannot?
cards.a: Epithelium regrows outwards from surviving hair follicles and sweat glands, which lie in the dermis. If the whole dermis is destroyed there are no appendages left to regenerate from, so the wound can only scar.
